title-group.article-title: Physical Activity Interventions Within Occupational Therapy for Persons With Substance Use Disorder: A Systematic Review

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding Author: Kayleigh Nolan, Division of Occupational Therapy, West Virginia University, PO Box 9139, 64 Medical Center Drive, Morgantown, WV 26505, USA. Kayleigh.swanson@hsc.wvu.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

abstract.p: Substance use disorders (SUDs) may significantly impact occupational performance and participation. Physical activity interventions are beneficial for individuals with SUD and can be delivered by occupational therapy practitioners (OTPs). We aimed to evaluate the current evidence for physical activity interventions within the scope of occupational therapy (OT) for treating SUDs. This systematic review followed PRISMA guidelines for data abstraction and assessment, including 28 articles in this review. Searches were conducted in Medline, APA PsycInfo, Embase, CINAHL, and OTSeeker from September 2021 to August 2023. Physical activity interventions included yoga, tai chi, comprehensive integrated approaches (CIA), high-intensity/circuit training, and music/dance interventions. Yoga, tai chi, CIA, and high-intensity exercise significantly reduced substance use and cravings. Tai chi, CIA, and dance/music interventions decreased stress and improved mental health and sleep outcomes. This review provides a foundation for advancing OTP-directed physical activity interventions in SUD-related rehabilitation and recovery.

abstract.title: How Occupational Therapy Practitioners Can Help People With Substance Use Disorder Incorporate Physical Activity Into Their Daily Lives
abstract.p: Existing evidence was reviewed to better understand physical activity options for individuals with substance use disorder and how occupational therapy practitioners can incorporate these activities into their client sessions. Previous research supports that aerobic exercise, physical activity, and group-based exercises can help reduce substance cravings, lower the risk of relapse, and improve mood and motivation. In this review, physical activities examined included yoga, tai chi, comprehensive integrated approaches (CIA), high-intensity or circuit training, and music and dance activities. There is strong evidence that physical activity enhances outcomes for individuals with substance use disorders. Occupational therapy practitioners can implement physical activity into their treatment sessions to support individuals in substance use rehabilitation and recovery through integrating walking, jogging, cardiovascular training, strength training, yoga, tai chi, and dance.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: This systematic review aims to inform occupational therapy practitioners (OTPs) about the importance of physical activity interventions in treating individuals with substance use disorder (SUD) and to present evidence supporting the integration of these interventions into occupational therapy (OT). According to the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework, 4th ed., physical activity includes “Completing cardiovascular exercise, strength training, and balance training to improve or maintain health and decrease the risk of health episodes, such as by incorporating walks into a daily routine” (AOTA, 2020, p. 32). In addiction treatment literature, physical activity (i.e., exercise) is a behavioral intervention that has been studied in both humans and animals.

SUD is recognized as a chronic medical condition marked by a persistent pattern of substance use despite ongoing negative consequences (Gutman, 2006). While conventional behavioral therapies for SUD remain central to treatment, they often fall short in sustaining long-term recovery, with abstinence rates averaging approximately 30% five years or more following treatment (White, 2012). These limitations underscore the need for innovative, interdisciplinary strategies that address both the physical and psychosocial dimensions of recovery. Physical activity is a promising behavioral intervention for individuals with SUD, warranting greater attention and application within OT practice.

sec.title: Evidence Supporting Physical Activity Interventions
sec.p: A systematic review found that 68.8% of exercise studies reported improved addiction-related outcomes, including reduced cravings, higher abstinence rates, and improved withdrawal symptoms with aerobic exercise interventions (Patterson et al., 2022). Studies using strength training and yoga/tai chi also reported improvements in addiction-related outcomes (Patterson et al., 2022). In addition to individualized intervention programs, there is evidence that group-based exercise for treating SUDs offers increased benefits due to the social and motivational support it provides (Lovell et al., 2016; Moore et al., 2012; Yorks et al., 2017).

The literature further supports that exercise can be particularly beneficial as an intervention for stimulant use disorder. He et al. (2021) found that exercise reduced amphetamine cravings, while Wang et al. (2014) and Wang et al. (2015) found that exercise reduced cravings in individuals with methamphetamine use disorder. In animal studies, nonweight-loaded exercise (Segat et al., 2022) and swimming (Rosa et al., 2020) have been shown to reduce amphetamine relapse. Improvements in mood and mental health are also linked to exercise interventions. Exercise has been shown to reduce anxiety and depression, improve sleep, and enhance cognitive function in individuals who use methamphetamines (Haglund et al., 2015; He et al., 2021; Mooney et al., 2014; Rawson, Chudzynski, Gonzales, et al., 2015; Rawson, Chudzynski, Mooney, et al., 2015). Regarding outcomes across various SUDs, exercise has been shown to reduce cravings and relapse rates for multiple substances (Berg et al., 2012; Brown et al., 2010; Horn et al., 2011; Weinstock et al., 2008). Studies by Lu et al. (2021) and Stimpson et al. (2018) have shown that exercise enhances cognitive measures, including responses to drug cues. Broadly, Abdullah and colleagues (2022) concluded in their review that “Exercise is beneficial for all types of drug addiction at all stages, by reversing molecular damage caused to dopaminergic and glutamatergic systems” (p. 306).

The mechanisms behind exercise’s positive effects include reducing neurotoxicity in the brain, particularly in relation to methamphetamine use, and promoting neurogenesis in the hippocampus. These effects improve cognitive function and help reduce drug-seeking behaviors (Somkuwar, 2015). From a neurobiological perspective, exercise is an effective treatment for SUD, partly because it decreases dopaminergic activity in the brain’s reward pathway during both acute and post-acute withdrawal phases. These phases are characterized by increased symptoms of anhedonia and cravings, which raise relapse risk (Gold et al., 2009). In addition, exercise has been shown to stabilize and upregulate dopaminergic signaling and glutamate levels, helping reduce withdrawal symptoms (Abdullah et al., 2022; Lynch et al., 2013; Rosa et al., 2020).

sec.title: Alignment With Occupational Therapy Practice
sec.p: Physical activity interventions for physical, psychosocial, and cognitive rehabilitation fall within the scope of OTPs (American Occupational Therapy Association [AOTA], 2020, 2021). Existing evidence supports OTPs in incorporating physical activity interventions with individuals with traumatic brain injury (Fields et al., 2022; Wheeler and Acord-Vira, 2023); Parkinson’s disease (Wood et al., 2022); stroke (Hildebrand et al., 2023); multiple sclerosis (Cunningham et al., 2022); cancer (Newman et al., 2024); developmental disabilities (Taylor et al., 2024); chronic pain (Schmid et al., 2019); and mental health challenges (Crooks et al., 2024; Hill et al., 2023; Nesbitt et al., 2021). Examples of physical activity interventions within OT literature include home exercise programs, activity diaries, functional motor and balance tests, cardiovascular training, and strength training (Fields et al., 2022); fitness-based exercise programs like walking and jogging (Ussher et al., 2000); and yoga and tai chi (Bradshaw, 2017; Crooks et al., 2024). OTPs are particularly effective in incorporating physical activity into their interventions due to their comprehensive consideration of client factors, performance skills, performance patterns, context, and the activity demands that influence client participation (AOTA, 2020, 2021; Bradshaw, 2017).

Although there is limited existing evidence from occupational therapy supporting physical activity interventions for individuals with substance use disorder (Ussher et al., 2000), OTPs have a long history of working with individuals who have substance use disorders (Amorelli, 2016; Ryan & Boland, 2021; Ryan et al., 2023; Wasmuth et al., 2014, 2015, 2016). OTPs use occupation as a tool for recovery and enhancing quality of life, recognizing that individuals with SUDs face challenges that hinder occupational performance (Stoffel & Moyers, 2004). The benefits of physical activity noted in the OT literature for individuals experiencing substance use include that physical activity can build health management skills, including developing and managing health and wellness routines (Fields et al., 2022); serve as a means of motivation and structure in one’s day (Ussher et al., 2000); increase confidence in one’s body movements (Ussher et al., 2000); and enhance psychological well-being by reducing attention on substance use (Ussher et al., 2000). OTPs are uniquely capable of integrating physical activity interventions to foster meaningful engagement in daily life for individuals with SUD. This review provides a foundation for broadening OT practice to include evidence-based physical activity interventions that meet the complex needs of this population.

sec.title: Methodology
sec.p: This systematic review, adhering to PRISMA guidelines (Page et al., 2021), was conducted to identify studies that utilized physical activity and exercise interventions to treat individuals with SUDs. A health sciences librarian developed database search strategies for key concepts related to SUD and occupational therapy, utilizing a combination of database-specific subject headings and keywords. Keywords included: Substance use disorder; substance abuse; drug use; drug dependence; and substance-related diagnoses (i.e., opioid use disorder, stimulant use disorder). Subject headings searched in alignment with literature on occupational therapy included: Abstinence; reduction of cravings; mental health symptom reduction; activities of daily living; instrumental activities of daily living; health management; rest and sleep participation; work/education participation; play/leisure participation; social participation; and performance patterns (habits, routines, rituals, and roles). The search included five databases: Medline (Ovid), APA PsycInfo (Ovid), Embase (Embase.com), CINAHL (EBSCO), and OTSeeker, all of which were searched and reviewed from September 2021 to August 2023. A review protocol has been registered through the Open Science Framework (2021).

sec.title: Search Strategy and Study Selection Process
sec.p: The initial scoping review was intentionally broad, encompassing all empirical studies. It included interventional studies, randomized controlled trials, quasi-experimental pre–post designs, case studies, single-subject designs, and observational cohort studies, with keywords related to OT interventions and participant SUD diagnosis. There were no limits on the publication years in this initial review. Studies that were published in peer-reviewed journals or gray literature, including poster presentations, were considered. Subjects who were clinically diagnosed with or who selfreported substance use disorder, as defined previously, were also included in the large review. Studies that reported tobacco as the only substance used by participants were excluded from this review. Since there were no translators on the research team, only published articles or reports in English were considered. Interventions with adults aged 18 and over were included. Studies within both inpatient and outpatient settings were included for review. The initial retrieval identified 1,523 articles, which we then filtered using a narrower review criterion to achieve our review goals. Additional exclusion criteria for this narrower systematic review included qualitative articles, Level 4 or 5 articles, articles older than 20 years, low-quality RCTs and/or protocols, articles with unclear evaluation methods, and articles with no abstract in English. This yielded 509 articles. A list of all articles included and excluded is presented in this article (see Figure 1 for the PRISMA flowchart at the end of this section).

The second screening of articles excluded studies with no physical activity intervention meeting our criteria, interventions not within the OT scope of practice, and studies with participants who had no qualifying SUD diagnosis. This screening yielded 86 articles. Physical activity intervention was defined using the “Occupational Therapy Practice Framework, 4th ed.” (AOTA, 2020), as previously stated.

According to AOTA (2020), occupations include activities of daily living (ADLs), instrumental activities of daily living (IADLs), health management, rest and sleep participation, work/education participation, play and leisure participation, and social participation. The skills needed to perform occupations are referred to as performance skills. OT interventions that included behavior skills, performance skills, biofeedback, and neurofeedback were included in the initial review, as these interventions affect the quality of the occupations being completed. However, if these were the only interventions used in the study, these articles were excluded from the final review as they were not considered physical activity interventions.

sec.title: Data Extraction and Risk of Bias
sec.p: After conducting a comprehensive review and a more focused systematic review of articles assessing physical activity interventions in the context of SUD treatment, 28 articles were identified for data analysis. Each of the articles reviewed was assigned a type of evidence score from Level 1 to Level 5, with Levels 4 and 5 being excluded from the analysis (OCEBM Levels of Evidence Working Group, 2009).

The risk of bias was assessed for each article individually, depending on the research design (Chandler et al., 2016; National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute, 2014; Shea et al., 2017). The primary author completed the first risk of bias assessment for each of the individual articles, and the remainder of the team checked for accuracy. There was 100% agreement among the team on the articles’ risk of bias assessments. These were used to help determine the strength of evidence for each of the physical activity intervention themes, categorized as strong, moderate, and low.

sec.title: Data Synthesis
sec.p: A qualitative approach was used to analyze the data by examining the studies for commonalities across different types of physical activity interventions used to treat SUD. The studies were then analyzed using an adapted strategy from the Agency for Health care Research and Quality U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (2012) to determine the strength of evidence or level of certainty for each intervention theme. A strong strength of evidence was assigned to an intervention theme with two or more Level 1 studies that were consistent and well-designed. A moderate strength of evidence was assigned to intervention themes with one Level 1 study or multiple moderate quality studies, including Level 2 and 3. A low level of evidence was assigned to intervention themes with a small number of low-level studies, indicating the studies were small, had flaws in design or methods, were inconsistent, and/or lacked sufficient information.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Abstracts of 86 articles were reviewed, with 28 full articles included in this review (see Table 1 for evidence levels across interventions at the end of the results section). The articles evaluated physical activity interventions aimed at developing, managing, and maintaining health and wellness routines, including self-management, to improve health and support participation in daily activities for individuals with SUDs. The physical activities examined in the studies included yoga (n = 10), tai chi (n = 7), high-intensity and circuit training (n = 3), comprehensive integrated approaches (n = 6), and music and/or dance interventions (n = 2).

sec.title: Yoga Interventions
sec.p: Yoga was the most common physical activity intervention in this review. Ten articles focused solely on yoga as the intervention, 6 Level 1 and 4 Level 2 studies (Agarwal et al., 2015; Gaihre & Rajesh, 2018a, 2018b; Hallgren et al., 2014; Lander et al., 2018; Petker et al., 2021; Sharma & Singh, 2017; Sureka et al., 2014; Willy-Gravley et al., 2021; Wimberly et al., 2018). Across the studies, yoga interventions varied in terms of intensity, dosage, and style of yoga. The intervention sessions ranged from 1 to 6 sessions/week, each lasting 60–90 minutes, and had a duration of 5 to 12 weeks. The yoga interventions included a warmup, meditation, breathing and relaxation techniques, and yoga body movements. All the studies showed statistically significant benefits of yoga as an intervention for individuals with SUDs. Regarding overall health and well-being, yoga was effective in reducing anxiety (Sureka et al., 2014) and stress (Agarwal et al., 2015; Lander et al., 2018; Wimberly et al., 2018), as well as improving quality of life (QOL; Agarwal et al., 2015), general health, and psychological health (Sureka et al., 2014). Yoga was found to be effective in reducing somatic and psychiatric symptoms, as well as substance consumption (Wimberly et al., 2018) and drug cravings (Petker et al., 2021). People who participated in yoga demonstrated less impulsivity and showed improved mindfulness, including the ability to notice, not worry, regulate attention, self-regulate their emotions, and trust their body (Petker et al., 2021). In terms of physical health outcomes, yoga has been found to be effective in improving finger dexterity, digit span, tapping speed, and range of motion (Gaihre & Rajesh, 2018a, 2018b). In one study with incarcerated females, the authors concluded that yoga may be an effective integrative treatment for mental health and substance use to address warnings of relapse, emotional regulation, and body dissociation (Willy-Gravley et al., 2021). There is strong evidence to support yoga as an effective intervention to reduce stress and anger and improve overall health and well-being for individuals with SUDs.

sec.title: Tai Chi Interventions
sec.p: There were a total of seven articles that included tai chi, 4 Level 1 and 3 Level 2 articles (He et al., 2021; Li et al., 2013; Menglu et al., 2021; Oh & Kim, 2016; Zhang & Zhu, 2020; Zhu et al., 2016, 2018). Tai chi in these studies was modified and varied, with groups occurring between 1 and 5 sessions per week for 1 to 6 months. Each session included a 10-minute warm-up, 30–40 minutes of tai chi interventions, and a 10-minute cool-down.

Tai chi interventions were found to be effective at improving QOL (Zhu et al., 2018), overall sense of health (Zhang & Zhu, 2020), sleep quality (Zhu et al., 2018), psychological effect (Li et al., 2013), mental health (e.g., anger, depression, anxiety; Oh & Kim, 2016; Zhang & Zhu, 2020; Zhu et al., 2018), and overall physical fitness including balance (Zhu et al., 2016) and vital signs (e.g., blood pressure, heart rate; Menglu et al., 2021; Zhang & Zhu, 2020; Zhu et al., 2018). It was also effective at improving reaction time, sensitivity to drug-related words (He et al., 2021), and decreasing drug cravings (Oh & Kim, 2016; Zhang & Zhu, 2020). Executive functioning, specifically in terms of reaction time and accuracy (Menglu et al., 2021), also improved with tai chi intervention. There is strong evidence supporting the use of tai chi interventions for individuals with SUDs to improve overall health.

sec.title: Comprehensive Integrated Approaches
sec.p: Six articles included comprehensive integrated approaches as interventions, comprised of 3 Level 1 and 3 Level 3 articles (Kumar et al., 2020, 2021; Przekop et al., 2018; Ross et al., 2008; Tamhankar et al., 2005; Zhu et al., 2020). These articles included a combination of physical activity interventions aimed at improving recovery from SUDs. In two of the studies, an integrated intervention for alcohol use disorder included cognitive remediation and mind-body exercises such as qigong and tai chi for 18 days. These studies were effective in enhancing relaxation and attentiveness, while also decreasing stress, hyperarousal (Kumar et al., 2021), and risk-taking and impulsivity (Kumar et al., 2020). In addition, they were found to be effective in remediating neurocognitive dysfunction such as conflict monitoring, response inhibition, and reward processing.

One study involved a multimodal pain management program that combined education and physical activity. The intensity was 15.5 hours per week and included a 12-step program focused on medical detoxification, group education, and osteopathic recovery-based SUD treatment. The intervention was effective at improving depression, pain, and QOL (Przekop et al., 2018). Ross and colleagues (2008) also studied a comprehensive integrated intervention approach combining cognitive behavioral therapy with alternative therapies (music, art, yoga, meditation, anger management, and trauma group therapies), which demonstrated improvements in psychopathological and psychological symptoms (Ross et al., 2008). Tamhankar and colleagues (2005) found that participants in a holistic therapeutic intervention program, including detoxification, yoga, and counseling, were more likely to be sober at 3 months and 1 year when attending AA meetings.

One study utilized a Mind-Body Exercise Group that consisted of combined movements from qigong, tai chi, and yoga (Zhu et al., 2020). The sessions consisted of nine movements, each completed four times, and were repeated twice. This was practiced three times a day—morning, afternoon, and evening. This intervention was effective at improving QOL in drug addiction, physical fitness outcomes, and vital signs (e.g., blood pressure, pulse). Overall, there is strong evidence to support the use of comprehensive integrated approaches with a combination of physical exercise, counseling, pain management, and cognitive rehabilitation as interventions for individuals with SUDs.

sec.title: High-Intensity and Circuit Training Interventions
sec.p: Three articles, two Level 1 and one Level 2, included high-intensity and circuit training interventions for people with SUDs (Bichler et al., 2017; Yan-Guang et al., 2021; Yuvarani et al., 2019). In the first study, a Nordic-walking (NW) condition was compared with a control group and a yoga-gymnastics (YG) condition. Although no between-group differences were found for craving or heart rate, acute exercise improved affective responses with a preference for NW over YG conditions (Bichler et al., 2017). Two of the studies specifically examined intensity training, including relaxation and breathing exercises, with varying intensities: 1 hour/week, 3 times/week for 12 months, and then progressing to 5 days/week, 2 times/day for 45 days (Yan-Guang et al., 2021; Yuvarani et al., 2019).

Yan-Guang and colleagues (2021) found significant changes within groups for vital signs, hand grip, sit-and-reach, one-leg standing with eyes closed, and reaction time, as well as decreased cravings, indicating that physical activity is beneficial. However, there were no significant differences between groups regarding fitness or cravings. Yuvarani and colleagues (2019) found a statistically significant difference (p < .001) where the intervention group had a greater reduction in the signs and symptoms of alcohol withdrawal. These interventions produced improvements in terms of cravings, affective valence, and vital signs (e.g., heart rate, blood pressure). Overall, there is moderate evidence to support the use of high-intensity, circuit training interventions for individuals with SUDs.

sec.title: Music and Dance Interventions
sec.p: Two articles that included music and dance interventions in this review, 1 Level 2 and 1 Level 3 (Cevasco et al., 2005; Dempsey, 2009). The music therapy program included three different types of physical activity interventions: Movement-to-music activities, rhythm activities, and competitive games, all conducted for one hour, twice a week, over a two-week period (Cevasco et al., 2005). Participants who completed the study demonstrated improvements in anger, anxiety, worry/oversensitivity, and social concerns/stress. The dance movement group interventions included Chacian techniques that focused on socialization and communication within the group. The study found a significant decrease in anxiety, worry, and stress (Dempsey, 2009). Because there are few articles on this intervention topic, there is limited evidence to support using dance and music for people with SUDs. Still, finding ways to include enjoyable social activities seems to be a promising approach that could help reduce anxiety in individuals with SUDs.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: As the overdose epidemic continues to grow, impacting individuals, families, and communities, innovative interdisciplinary models of treatment are needed now more than ever. This systematic review summarizes the existing literature highlighting the role that OTPs can play in enhancing overall health and substance use outcomes in individuals with SUDs. Rehabilitation needs often include physical, cognitive, and psychosocial components that may not be comprehensively addressed in traditional addiction treatment programs. Building on current knowledge about the effectiveness of physical activity interventions for treating SUDs and the clinical presentation of individuals with long-term substance use, the skill set of OTPs align with the needs of people with SUD to achieve long-term recovery and reintegration into daily life.

sec.title: Results Summary
sec.p: The results of this systematic review, examining the current literature on physical activity interventions in the treatment of individuals with SUDs, are overwhelmingly positive. All the studies that used yoga as an intervention had statistically significant findings regarding drug use outcomes, including reducing substance use and cravings. Studies examining tai chi interventions have also shown positive results, including reduced drug cravings, improved sleep, and fewer mental health symptoms. Comprehensive integrated models that combine physical activity and mindfulness interventions demonstrated a positive effect on neurocognitive dysfunction and were linked to improved substance use outcomes, such as reduced drug use, lower stress, decreased hyperarousal, and improved mental health. High-intensity exercise has been shown to decrease substance cravings and enhance emotional well-being. While the dance and music interventions in this review did not lead to significant reductions in SUD outcomes, they were effective in reducing anxiety and stress.

Individuals in early recovery from SUDs often present with co-occurring conditions such as depressed mood, chronic pain, fatigue, and medical co-morbidities, making physical activity particularly challenging. This is where OTP strengths lie, given their training in working with individuals with both acute and chronic health conditions and creating person, task, and/or environmental adaptations and modifications to enhance participation (AOTA, 2020). This may involve adapting interventions, such as introducing graded functional mobility that incorporates mindfulness and grounding techniques for individuals experiencing anxious or depressed moods. Additional adaptations include using seated instead of standing yoga or tai chi poses to accommodate pain and physical limitations and integrating physical activity into existing daily routines by adding reaching and lifting activities during participation. Physical activity interventions are of benefit at all stages of the SUD recovery continuum, helping to modulate neurotransmitter dysregulation to address craving and negative mood states during the acute and post-acute withdrawal phase. In addition, physical activity is beneficial for improving cognitive function and enhancing learning, which can help prevent relapse in ongoing recovery (Abrantes & Blevins, 2019).

sec.title: Implications for Occupational Therapy Practitioners
sec.p: The use of physical activity through activities of daily living, therapeutic activity, and exercise by OTPs to support recovery and promote health is well established. Integrating these activities into an individual’s daily routine offers the added benefits of forming a productive habit, a commonly cited factor in sustained SUD recovery (Kitzinger et al., 2023). Epley and colleagues (2021) emphasize the importance of building habits and daily routines in SUD treatment through occupations such as work, leisure, and activities of daily living. Combining the known benefits of physical activity with an occupational profile that reflects an individual’s values, interests, and priorities may provide an ideal foundation for client-centered occupational therapy treatment, facilitating the integration of routines, behaviors, and habits into everyday environments (Epley et al., 2021).

To effectively integrate physical activity into their scope of practice, OTPs may need support across clinical, organizational, and educational domains. From a broader perspective, advocating for flexible documentation and billing structures, leadership that supports holistic and innovative care, and access to appropriate equipment and environments can empower OTPs to integrate physical activity meaningfully into their intervention plans. Furthermore, pursuing professional development opportunities related to physical activity intervention, such as graded or adaptive techniques, and interprofessional collaboration, including working with physical therapists, psychologists, and social workers, can help OTPs address the complex needs of clients, especially those with co-occurring physical and mental health conditions.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: The systematic review employed multiple approaches to ensure rigor and accuracy, including a rigorous multi-database search strategy, adherence to PRISMA guidelines, and risk of bias assessments. However, while clearly supporting the positive contributions of physical activity to SUD recovery, several limitations in the systematic review should be noted. Although many of the studies in this review fell within the scope of occupational therapy, most were not conducted by OTPs. As a result, role delineation for occupational therapy cannot be clearly established. In addition, differences in methodology and statistical analysis among studies reviewed impact homogeneity and, subsequently, the strength of the derived results and conclusions (Shaheen et al., 2023).

sec.title: Future Directions
sec.p: Future research is needed to clarify further the unique role of occupational therapy in SUD recovery. Ongoing studies in this area should examine the nature of the relationship between SUD and activities of daily living, develop functional assessment measures for SUD, and describe occupation-based strategies for integrating physical activity and other interventions into clinical practice across various settings for individuals with SUD.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This systematic review has yielded strong evidence that physical activity interventions improve outcomes for individuals with SUDs. The current overdose and SUD crisis calls for an all-hands-on-deck approach to treatment. This review (a) seeks to highlight effective physical activity interventions that fall within the skill set of OTPs and (b) to elicit a response from OTPs to build up evidence for the effectiveness of physical activity interventions administered by OTPs to individuals with SUD. OTPs have expertise in the delivery of client-centered interventions that have been shown to improve a variety of physical, psychological, and cognitive symptoms associated with SUDs. This conclusion leads to the natural next step—the need to investigate the integration of such interventions as components of current multimodal approaches to SUD treatment. Furthermore, this next step in understanding the impact of occupational therapy interventions needs to include an investigation of efficacy in individuals with differing SUD severity and different SUD diagnoses. According to the CDC, U.S. overdose deaths continue to increase each year, with 109,680 deaths in 2022 being the highest ever reported (CDC, 2023), so time is of the essence to develop new innovative models of treatment. Physical activity interventions show excellent promise.

Providing OTP-directed physical activity interventions can broaden the support available to individuals with SUDs, including individuals who desire and benefit from more holistic, person-centered treatment options, as well as enhance their overall health and well-being (Ryan et al., 2023). By providing this type of intervention, individuals with SUDs can be exposed to new leisure, health, and occupational pursuits that they can integrate into their lives in recovery.

ack.p: The authors thank the West Virginia University Division of Occupational Therapy for providing graduate students to assist with early article screening. They also wish to acknowledge the University of Pittsburgh Health Sciences Library System for supporting Ms. Turner.

sec.p: The authors disclosed receipt of the following financial support for the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article: This work was, in part, supported by funding from the National Institutes of Health (UG3 DA 049436), which supported Ms. Lander and Dr. Winstanley as part of the National Institute on Drug Abuse’s (NIDA) Clinical Trials Network (CTN).

fn.p: Ethical Approval and Informed Consent Statement
Ethical approval and informed consent were not required for this systematic review.
The authors declared no potential conflicts of interest with respect to the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: Review data will be shared upon request.

caption.p: PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) Flowchart
Evidence for the Effectiveness of Physical Activity Interventions for Persons With Substance Use Disorder.

tr.th: Level of evidence, studydesign, and risk of bias

tr.td: Yoga Interventions
Level 2Pilot StudyRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 24 (Intervention M age, 47 years; 33% female) (Control M age, 49.3; 42% female) Inclusion CriteriaEnglish speaking, HIV diagnosis, cocaine use Intervention Setting Medical Wellness Center

Intervention 1: Yoga and meditation (YM; n = 12), 2× week, 60 mins for 2 months. Groups included yogic techniques (i.e., warm-up yogic body movements, yogic breathing techniques, meditation, yogic asana, and sava asana [yoga Nidra] for relaxation).Control Group (n = 12)No contact
Short Form-36 (SF-36)Perceived Stress Scale (PSS)Impact of Events Scale (IES)Salivary Cortisol and DHEA-SQuality of Life (QOL)
YM participants showed modest improvements on QOL.The PSS totals score and the IES intrusion score improved significantly 2 months after the intervention, but cortisol and DHEA-S did not change
Gaihre & Rajesh (2018b)
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 66 (Intervention 1 M age, 24.25 years; Intervention 2 M age, 24.77 years)Inclusion CriteriaMales with diagnosis of SUD, 18–40 years of age, stable psychologicalsymptoms, 3 weeks of sobriety or abstinenceIntervention Setting Residential Rehabilitation

Intervention 1: Yoga group (n = 33), in addition to inpatient rehabilitation, 6 sessions for 90 minutes per week for 12 weeks. The protocol consisted of standing, sitting, supine, and prone posture, breathing and relaxation techniques, and meditation.Intervention 2: Exercise group (n = 33), 6 sessions for 90 minutes per week for 12 weeks. The protocol consisted of loosening, warm-up, stretching, and moderate aerobics.
Finger Tapping TaskO’Connor TweezerDexterity TestAutomatic Mirror Tracer

Significant improvement from pre/post with tapping speeds 10s in the yoga (p < .05) and exercise (p < .026) groups, tapping speeds 20s in the yoga (p < .0036) and exercise (p < .032) groups, tweezer dexterity in the yoga and exercise (p < .001) groups, and a significant reduction in mirror time in the yoga (p < .034) and exercise (p < .0006) groups.Between groups, no significant differences in any of the motor outcomes.
Gaihre & Rajesh (2018a)
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Low

ParticipantsN = 87 (Intervention 1 M age, 25.18 years; Intervention 2 M age, 25.02 years)Inclusion Criteria18–40 years of age; substance use disorder, English speaking, stability in psychological symptoms (e.g., actively suicidal, psychotic), and a regulated substance intoxicationIntervention Setting Residential rehabilitation treatment unit

Intervention 1: Yoga (n = 44) Six weekly sessions, 90-minute duration over 12 weeks. Yoga module consisted of loosening practices, yogic postures, breathing exercises, meditation and relaxation techniquesIntervention 2: Physical Exercise program (n = 43) Six weekly sessions, 90-minute duration over 12 weeks Included loosening warm-up, moderate aerobics, stretching and strengthening exercises.
Stroop Color-Word TestWAIS-R Digit Span TaskSix Letter Cancelation Task

A significant enhancement in digit forward (yoga p < .0005, d = 0.81; exercise p < .0005, d = 0.73), digit backward (yoga p < .0005, d = 0.88; exercise p < .0005, d = 0.58), and letter cancelation test scores (yoga p < .0005, d = 1.31; exercise p < .0005, d = 1.4) were observed in both the yoga and exercise groups.
Level 2Pilot StudyRisk of Bias Moderate
ParticipantsN = 14Inclusion CriteriaAlcohol Dependence (AD), 18 years of age or older.Intervention Setting Outpatient alcohol treatment clinic

Intervention 1: Yoga (n = 8)Weekly yoga group sessions, psychological and pharmacological interventions for AD, 1.5 hours for 10 weeks. Included breathing techniques, light physical exercise, meditation and deep relaxation, and continued exercises at home.Control Group: (n = 6) Treatment as usual included counseling sessions with CBT and/or motivational interviewing and appropriate pharmacological interventions for AD.
Short Alcohol Dependence DataQuestionnaireHospital Anxiety and Depression Scale Sheehan Disability ScalePerceived Stress Scale and SalivaCortisol
Alcohol consumption decreased more in the yoga group as compared with treatment as usual, but not statistically significant (p = .17).
Level 2Pilot StudyRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 26Inclusion CriteriaOpioid use disorder within the last 12 months; active in the clinic-based buprenorphine/naloxone medication-assisted treatment (MAT) program for more than 90 days; male or a non-pregnant, female age 18 or greater; EnglishIntervention Setting Outpatient Clinic

Intervention 1: Yoga (n = 13) Weekly yoga intervention for 12 weeks, Vinyasa flow movements, restorative yoga poses, mindful breathing, and meditations. Participants remained in MAT.Control Group: (n = 13)Treatment as Usual Group, attended MAT and CBT based group therapy
Perceived Stress ScaleOpioid Craving ScaleSleep ScaleAnxiety Scale

For perceived stress there were no significant differences at baseline (p = .674) or 45 days (p = .631) follow-up, but the 90 day group difference was significant with perceived stress much lower for the MAT+yoga group (p = .008). All other group difference by time was not significant.
Level 2Controlled Trial withoutRandomizationRisk of Bias Moderate
ParticipantsN = 75 (M age, 37.7 years, 100% female)Inclusion CriteriaAdult females enrolled in WomankindAddiction ServicesIntervention Setting Residential inpatient program for adult women with SUD

Intervention 1: Yoga (n = 42) A 5-week program (144 hours) including Treatment as usual (TAU) plus the Trauma-informed yoga intervention. TAU included the following: exploration of self, identifying maladaptive behaviors, relationship patters, coping skills, codependency, emotional regulation and self-efficacy, self-acceptance, relapse prevention, social support. In addition to TAU, clients participated in Yoga Fit for Warriors, 1-hour sessions, 3 days/week for 14 sessions.Control Group: (n = 33)Treatment as Usual

NIDA-modified version of the World Health Organization’s Alcohol Smoking Substance Involvement Screening Test (ASSIST).Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) section on the Generalized AnxietyDisorder (GAD-7) and Somatic SymptomPost Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)ChecklistPenn Craving Scale (PCS)Brief Situational Confidence Questionnaire (BSCQ)Impulsive Behavior Scale (UPPS-P)Monetary Choice Questionnaire (MCQ)

Significant improvement in the treatment as usual plus yoga group with range of motion (p = .06) and Lack of Premeditation facet of impulsivity (p = .04)For both groups, statistically significant (p < .05) within subjects’ changes were seen with somatic and psychiatric symptoms, drug cravings, self-efficacy, impulsivity (negative urgency, lack of premeditation) and mindfulness (noticing, not worrying, attention regulation, self-regulation, body listening, trusting)
Sharma & Singh (2017)
LevelRCTRisk of Bias Moderate
ParticipantsN = 30 malesInclusion criteriaMale, 15 to 35 years old, drug useIntervention settingTwo drug rehabilitation centers

Intervention 1: Yoga (n = 10)Exercises focusing on physical poses, breathing techniques, chanting and concertation for 10 weeks, 5 sessions/week for 1 hour.Intervention 2: Recreational activity (n = 10) Including cat and mince, changing seats, singing, drop the handkerchief, elimination ball, dance, keep the ball, duck walk race, tug of war, leapfrog, hill dill, busy bee, sound and action, leg cricket, for 10 weeks, 5 sessions/week for 1 hour.Control Group: (n = 10)No training
Criminal Prosperity (measures aggression, obedience, anti-social behavior, and drug use).

Significant difference between the adjusted mean score of yoga and the control group (p = .001), and between the adjusted mean score of recreational activity and the control group (p = 000).No significant difference between the adjusted mean scores of yoga and recreational activity indicating they are equal at reducing criminal prosperity, however when means were adjusted, recreational activity was more effective.
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Low

ParticipantsN = 111 (Intervention 1 M age, 39.3 years; Control M age, 38.8 years); 0% female) Inclusion CriteriaMale subjects with Substance Dependence, aged 18–65 yearsIntervention Setting Central Jail Hospital (CJH) de-addiction ward

Intervention 1: Yoga (n = 55)Intensive 6-week program in Sudarshan Kriya Yoga (SKY), daily including breathing practices (P) (arm postures, either done in a rapid or rhythmic pattern. Continued to receive pharmacological therapy that was unchanged during the study.Control Group: (n = 56)No training

Basic Socio-demographic PerformaMini Mental State Examination (MMSE)Severity of Dependence Scale (SDS)Schedule for Clinical Assessment in Neuropsychiatry (SCAN)Based clinical interviewGlobal Assessment of Functioning (GAF)Psychological General Well Being (PGWB)

Practicing Sudarshan Kriya Yoga (SKY) and related practices (P) helps in improving Global Assessment of Functioning, Psychological General Well Being, General Health (GH) and positive well-being of an individual. SK&P also causes significant reduction in anxiety levels of an individual.After six weeks of SK&P, statistically significant improvement in study subject dimensional scores of GAF, Anxiety, Depressed Mood (DEP), Positive Well Being (PWB), General Health (GH) and Total PGWB were noticed. When compared with control subjects, improvement was statistically significant in the case of GAF (p = .000158), ANX (p = .011), PWB (p = .02), GH (p = .02) and Total PGWB (p = .05); but not in the case of DEP, SC and Vitality.
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 114 (100% female)Inclusion CriteriaPast substance use history, nonparticipation in past EMBER yoga programs, and early in the treatment processIntervention Setting Midwestern correctional center

Intervention 1: EMBER yoga (n = 35), 6 weeks, 2×/week for 90-minutes, all sessions assessed its effects on emotion regulation, body dissociation, and warnings of substance use relapse.Intervention 2: Therapeutic community (n = 43)Control Group: (n = 35)No intervention
Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale (DERS)Scale of Body Connection (SBC)Advanced Warnings of Relapse (AWR)

An analysis of covariance revealed significant differences in warnings of relapse (p < .01), emotion regulation (p < .01), and body dissociation (p < .05). Paired samples t tests revealed significant change from pre- to posttest for the intervention group across all outcome variables. Findings suggest that yoga may be an effective integrative treatment for mental health and substance use challenges among incarcerated females.
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 73 (Intervention M age, 43.30, 18.82% female; Control M age, 45.61, 38.89% female)Inclusion Criteria18 years of age or older, English speaking, a client of the service provider, HIV diagnosis, problematic substance use or dependence, released from prison or jail in previous 12 months.Intervention Setting Service provider location

Intervention 1: Haga yoga (n = 37), 90-minutes weekly, 12 sessions focused on teaching coping skills in conjunction with breathing exercises and physical posture to enhance adaptive responses to stressful situations and use the stress-management strategies to reduce the risk of substance use.Control Group: (n = 36)Treatment as usual (case management, recreational or GED classes, free healthcare)
Perceived Stress Scale (assess stress)Timeline Follow back (assesses substance use) completed at one-month, two months, and three months
Yoga group was found to be significant for decreasing stress (p < .05) and less days of substance use than the control group at one, two, and three months (p < .001).
Tai Chi Interventions
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 32 (M age, 42.34 years; 0% Female)Inclusion Criteria Between the ages of 20–65 years old, currently in compulsory isolation and substance rehabilitation, SUD diagnosis, no brain injury, no history of mental illness, normal color vision, normal intelligence, elementary education or above, can participate in Tai Chi without physical disabilityIntervention SettingNot reported

Intervention 1: Tai chi exercise group (n = 16) Included 24 simplified trainings, 3×/week for 4 weeks. Each session was 45 minutes (warm up = 10, practice = 25, cooldown = 10). First week = training on basics of Tai Chi, remaining 3 weeks focused on consolidating the learning.Control Group: (n = 16)Daily Exercise (radio gymnastics and free-hand exercises). Frequency same as Tai Chi group
Cravings Assessment using a Visual Analog Scale (VAS).Color-Word Stroop Task (assess attention bias)
Significant improvement in the Tai Chi group with faster reaction time (p < .05) and a smaller index of sensitivity (p < .05) to drug related and neutral words.
Level2 RCTRisk of Bias Low
ParticipantsN = 33 (M age, 29.87; 100% female)Inclusion CriteriaOlder than 18, diagnosis of opioid dependence, no serious diseaseIntervention SettingNot reported

Intervention 1: Tai Chi Group (n = 17), every two days for 6 months. Group included 10 minutes of warmup, 1 hour of Tai Chi supervised by a coach, 10 minutes of cool-down exerciseIntervention 2: (n = 16)Similar levels of exercise excluding tai chiControl Group: (n = 18)Age matched, no intervention, healthy adult females
Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HRSD)Rating Scale for Heroine Withdrawal Symptoms

There was a small significant difference at 60-day mark between the Tai Chi and Control groups for psychological effects, but not statistically significant at 3 or 6 months. Both groups were also compared with age matched healthy individuals that suggested a deleterious effect of drug addiction.
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Low

ParticipantsN = 72 (Intervention M age, 39.31 years; Control M age, 39.37 years, 100% female) Inclusion Criteria Female, aged 18–65, reported history of Methamphetamine Dependence, educational attainment of primary school or above.Intervention Setting Shanghai Female Mandatory Detox & Rehabilitation Center (SFMDRC)

Intervention 1: Tai Chi (TC; n = 35), exercise group, 3×/40-minute sessions per week for 12 weeks in the afternoon on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday in the SFMDRC. Each session consisted of a 5-minute warmup, 30-minute exercise, and 5-minute cooldown.Control Group: (n = 37) Conventional exercises including the 9th Guang Bo TI Cao and square dance held at same time as intervention group.

Go/No-Go Task (assess executive functioning [EF] of inhibitory control (IC) and cognitive flexibility)Switching Tasks (assess executive functioning [EF] of inhibitory control (IC) and cognitive flexibility)3-Back Task (assess working memory)

The exercise group showed decreased response time (RT) with a significant main effect of time on the go/no-go task (p < .05).In the exercise group, the interaction effect between time and group was significant on accuracy (p < .05], The main effect of time was significant on RT (p < .05] in the 3-back task of the exercise group.Significant changes within the Tai chi exercise group on physical fitness with vital capacity and systolic blood pressure (p < .05), and BMI (p < .01).
Level 2Controlled Trial withoutRandomizationRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 38 (Intervention M age, 45.5 years; Control M age, 48.1yr)Inclusion CriteriaAlcohol dependence (AD), receiving inpatient treatment for AD a smoker; in the contemplation or preparation stage of change.Intervention Setting Psychiatric Hospital

Intervention 1: Tai Chi (n = 19) 24-posture yang style Tai Chi for 50 minutes 3×/week for 8 weeks in addition to routine hospital rehabilitation program.Control Group: (n = 19) Treatment as usual in the hospital rehabilitation program, exercise group. 3 sessions for 50 min/week for 8/weeks.
Stage of change in exercise on transtheoretical modelBlood Serotin LevelKorean version of the Beck InventoryState Trait Anger Expression Inventory-Korean Version

The experimental group showed a significantly increased blood serotonin level (p = .001) and significantly reduced nicotine dependence, depression, and anger (p = .001) than the control group did after 8 weeks of treatment
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Low

ParticipantsN = 76 (M age, 40 years; 0% female)Inclusion CriteriaAmphetamine (ATS) dependence, entering psych rehab, isolation period less than 12 months, no history of Taijiquan. Intervention Setting Brigade of a compulsory isolation detoxification center in Shanghai

Intervention 1: Taijiquan group (n = 38), exercise intervention in addition to routine rehabilitation exercise for 6 months, 1×/day, 5 days/week, tutoring teaching on technical level of the exercise, 40-minute intendent exercises alongside management police. 90% attendance rate Outcome measures completed at baseline, 3 and 6 months.Control Group: (n = 38)Routine rehabilitation exercise (gymnastics, sign language, queue exercises), received same exercises and content as the yoga group
National Physical Fitness Test Tool and QuestionnaireBaker self-rating depression scaleState-Trait Anxiety ScaleHealth Status SurveyAmphetamine craving scale

Statistically significant for the Taijiquan exercise group on promoting balance (p = .014), increased overall sense of health (p = .029), vitality (p = .030), mental health (p = .016), improved trait anxiety (p = .028), reduced drug craving (p = .048).
Level 1Study Design: Randomized Controlled TrialRisk of Bias Low

ParticipantsN = 80 (Intervention M age, 33.74; Control M age, 37.76; 80% female)Inclusion Criteria aged 18 years or above, level 3 SUD, including Amphetamine Dependence no severe medical conditions that would preclude their participation in physical activitiesIntervention Setting Clinic

Intervention 1: Tai Chi (TC) (TC; n = 42), 5×/week for first 3 months and 3×/week for second 3 months. TC group emphasized multidirectional weight shifting, awareness of body alignment and movement coordination. Each session consisted of a 10-minute warm-up, 40-minute TC exercise and 10-minute cooldownControl Group: Standard Care (SC) (SC; n = 38). The exercises in the SC group had similar exercise intensity as the experimental group, which included a 5-minute recreation activity (Guang Bo Ti Cao), 5-minute gesture language exercises and self-study.
Measured at baseline, 3 and 6 monthsPittsburgh Sleep Quality IndexSelf-Rating Depression ScaleFitness

The result showed that the PSQI scores of sleep duration, F(2, 92) = 9.86, need for sleep medications, F(2, 92) = 36.44, and daytime dysfunction, F(2, 92) = 5.15, were found to have a significant difference by time × group interaction after 6 months.SDS showed no significant difference between the two groups; however, the score of SDS in TC decreased after 6-month intervention, and no changes were observed in SC. Pulse rate had significantly decreased in the TC group compared with the SC group after 6 months. 9.5% (4) of participants in TC and 26.3% (10) of participants in SC were found to have relapsed.
Level 2Pilot StudyRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 60 (Intervention 1 M age 37.74 years; Control M age, 42.69; 0% female)Inclusion CriteriaAged 18 years or above; t drug-dependence, stimulants dependence; sedentary, that is, exercising < 20 min/day, 3 days a week for the past three months; and absence of severe medical conditions that would preclude participation in physical activitiesIntervention Setting Substance abuse detox and rehab center

Intervention 1: Tai Chi (n = 30). The Tai Chi intervention was 5×/week for 12 weeks and included 10 minutes of warm-up, 30 minutes of Tai Chi practice, and 10 minutes of cool-down exercises.Control Group: (n = 30)Standard care including recreation activity, gesture language exercise, and self-education.
Quality of Life for Drug AddictionQuestionnaire (QOL-DA)Physical Evaluations:Body Mass IndexBody FatHand GripFlexibilityBalance

Test scores of the QOL-DA in the Tai Chi group significantly increased after 12 weeks in the following areas: physiology, 8.71 (p = .005), symptoms, 4.34 (p = .042), society, 15.79 (p < .001), and total score, 10.60 (p = .002). A post hoc test further revealed that quality of life improved in the Tai Chi group but not in the standard care group.Physical results showed a significant interaction with balance, F(1,56) = 6.92, p = .011,; participants in the Tai Chi group improved by 10 s while there was no change in the standard care group. Although there were no significant interactions in the fitness outcomes (i.e., hand-grip and sit-and-reach tests), the within-group factor displayed significant changes in body fat, F(1,56) = 27.79, p < .001, in both groups.
Comprehensive Integrative Approaches
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 50 (Intervention M age, 33.88 years; Control M age 33.47 years)Inclusion CriteriaAged 18–45 years, diagnosis of early onset alcohol dependence (before 25 years of age), one or more first-degree relatives with alcohol dependenceIntervention Setting Inpatient ward at Center for Addiction Medicine (CAM)

Intervention 1: Integrated Intervention Program for Alcoholism (IIPA; cognitive remediation and mind-body exercise such as QiGong and Tai Chi; n = 25), 18 days in addition to treatment as usual (pharmacology, group sessions 3×/week, without yoga).Control Group: (n = 25)Treatment as usual (pharmacology, yoga 6×/week, group sessions 3×/week).
Resting–state electroencephalograms (EEG’s)Event-related potentials (ERP’s)Outcome-related positivity (ORP)

Statistically significant difference between groups using electrophysiological findings including alpha, beta, theta, and delta, eye closure, and resting-state EEGs (p < .05). IIPA is effective at enhancing relaxation state, attentiveness, decreasing hyperarousal, and remediating neurocognitive dysfunction (conflict-monitoring, response inhibition, reward processing).
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 50 (Intervention M age, 34.28 years, Control M age, 34.08 years)Inclusion CriteriaEarly onset alcohol dependence before 25 years of age, family history of alcohol dependence.Intervention Setting Inpatient setting of Center for Addiction Medicine

Intervention 1: Integrated Intervention Program for Alcoholism (IIPA; n = 25)Cognitive remediation to improve executive functioning and mind-body exercise such as Qigong and Tai Chi Chuan to enhance affect regulation, reduce stress and enhance relaxationControl Group: (n = 25) Treatment as Usual (TAU) including pharmacotherapy, yoga (6 sessions/week) and group relapse prevention (3 sessions/week) for 18 days.
Barratt’s Impulsiveness Scale (assesses impulsivity)Modified Iowa Gambling Task (mIGT; decision making task reflecting reward processing skills)
The IIPA was statistically significant for reducing impulsivity and disadvantageous reward processing/risk taking, no significant changes in the TAU group.
Level 3Cohort StudyRisk of Bias Low

ParticipantsN = 150 (M age, 48.4 years s 52.8% female)Inclusion Criteria Patients admitted to the treatment center, diagnosis of SUD, evaluated for presence of pain for 3 or more months Intervention Setting Residential drug and alcohol treatment center

Intervention 1: Multimodal Chronic Pain program An abstinence/substance based 12-step program, The group focused on medical detoxification, group education, external/internal qigong, osteopathic recovery-based SUD treatment. 15.5 hours/week Patients also received 20hr/wk. of the 12-step based SUD treatment.
Beck Depression Inventory-IIBrief Pain InventoryWest Haven-Yale Multidimensional Pain InventoryMcGill Pain QuestionnaireGlobal Impression of Change Scale

Outcome measures were completed at first assessment day and then on days 30 and 45. The global impression of change scale was administered on day 45.Participants had significantly improved scores in depression, pain, and quality of life ratings (p < .05)
Level 3Pilot StudyRisk of Bias Low

ParticipantsN = 80 (M age, 37.9 years; 20% female)Inclusion CriteriaA SUD and non-SUD diagnosis (co-occurring mental illness)Intervention Setting Specialized inpatient dual diagnosis unit at a public hospital

Intervention 1: Integrated intensive psychoeducation, 12-step facilitated meetings that were peer led, a token economy contingency management reward system, individual and group treatments utilizing supportive psychotherapy, motivation enhancement therapy, and cognitive behavioral therapy. Also integrated complementary and alternative therapies including music and art therapy, specialized yoga, meditation, anger management, and trauma group therapies.
Addiction Severity Index (ASI)Clinical Global Impression Severity Scale (CGI)The stages of Change, Readiness, and Treatment: Eagerness Scale (SOCRATES)Brief Symptom Inventory (BSI)

Psychiatric Symptoms, general functioning, motivation, and music therapy improvements were statistically significant from admission to discharge with the BSI all subscales (p < .05), GAF (p = .001), CGI (p = .001), SOCRATES Taking Steps (p = .005) and music therapy appreciation (p = .001)Appreciation of music therapy was positively correlated with Taking Steps (r = .25, p = .026) and therapist appreciation was significantly correlated with both Ambivalence (r = .24, p = .031) and Taking Steps (r = .27, p = .015) subscales of the SOCRATES
Level 3Risk of Bias Low
ParticipantsN = 40Inclusion CriteriaAlcohol Dependence, Intervention Setting Kripa Rehabilitation Center (de-addiction center)

Intervention 1:In-house treatment module beginning with initial stage of detoxification, followed by 3 weeks program of Yoga and experiential counseling, followed by 4 weeks program at center; after discharge, was monitored at workplace 2×/week
Hamilton Depression ScalePsychoticism Scale

Depression-Recovered (away from alcohol for ~year or more): p = .37 Neurotic-Recovered (away from alcohol for ~year or more): p = .96Extravert/Introvert-Recovered (away from alcohol for ~year or more): p = 2.11 (indicated as significant)Lie Score-Recovered (away from alcohol for ~year or more): p = 1.11Psychotic-Recovered (away from alcohol for ~year or more): p = 2.46 (indicated as significant)
Level 1Single-Blind RCTRisk of Bias Low

ParticipantsN = 100 (0% female; Intervention M age, 32 years; Control M age, 30 years) Inclusion Criteria Receiving drug withdrawal treatment at SMDRC, aged 18 and over, reported to be amphetamine users, no severe medical conditions.Intervention Setting Shanghai MandatoryDetoxification and Rehabilitation Center (SMDRC)

Intervention 1: Mind-Body Exercise Group (MBE; n = 50)Movements from qigong, tai chi, and yoga. Included acupoint massage of traditional Chinese medicine.Movements in harmony with breathing and involved both physical and mental components. e. practiced 3×/day.Control Group: (n = 50)Conventional Exercise group (CE) included 5 mins of recreational activities (the ninth Guang Bo Ti Cao), 5 mins of gesture language exercises, and self-study. Duration of control group was same as intervention group with frequency, intensity, and duration.

Physical Fitness Tests (BP, BMI, one leg standing with eyes closed, progressive aerobic cardiovascular endurance run [PACER], HR)Quality of Life for Addicted Users (QOL-DA; measures physiology, psychology, society, and symptoms)

Statistically significant differences between groups for Mental Outcomes with physiological, physiology, society outcomes and Total Score on the QOL-DA (p < .01) for 6 months.Statistically significant differences were found in the time x group interaction for Physical Fitness Outcomes with sit-and-reach, PACER, and HR during PACER (p < .01).Statistically significant differences were found in the time x group interaction for physiological outcomes with systolic and diastolic BP, pulse, pulse (p < .01), BMI, and vital capacity (p < .05)
High-Intensity and Circuit Training Interventions
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 15 (M age, 47.3 years; 20% female)Inclusion CriteriaAges 18 to 65, diagnosed with alcohol dependence with clinical observable cravings, but currently abstinent without relapseIntervention Setting Psychiatric hospital

Three interventions with a washout period for 1 week.Intervention 1: Nordic-walking (NW) condition Moderate intensity outdoor walking on uneven terrain at self-regulated pace with instructions to “brisk walking” corresponding to intensity level of 11 to 14 on perceived exertion.Intervention 2: Yoga-Gymnastics (YG) Condition Hatha yoga training (practice of posture), controlled breathing, meditation with instructions to correct posture and told to perform exercise with intensity level of 11 to 14 on perceived exertion.Passive Control Group:Passive, sat in a gym and read magazines that were provided.
Feeling Scale (FS; assesses affective responses)Felt Arousal Scale (FAS; assesses affective responses)Alcohol Urge Questionnaire (AUQ)Heart Rate Variability (HRV)

A significantly higher increase in perceived activation during NW vs. PC (partial eta2 = 0.34) as well as during YG vs. PC (partial eta2 = 0.33). Affective valence significantly increased during NW vs. YG (partial eta2 = 0.34). For cravings and HRV, no significant differences between treatment conditions were found.Acute exercise improved affective responses, and preference toward NW over YG
Level 1RCTRisk of Bias Low

ParticipantsN = 86 (Intervention 1 M age, 33.65 years; Intervention 2 M age, 32.2 years; 0% female)Inclusion Criteria:Aged 18–40, diagnosis of methamphetamine (MA) dependence, treatment duration at Shanghai Compulsory Rehabilitation Center (SCRC) more than 1 year, no serious medical or mental illness, primary school or above education.Intervention SettingOutdoor basketball field or indoor gym

Intervention 1: High-intensity Interval Training (HIIT; n = 40).Included 1 hour intervention (10-minute warmup, 40-minute HIIT, 10-minute cool down), 3×/week for 12 months. Included circuit training with a work-to-recovery ratio of 1:0.5.Intervention 2: Moderate intensity continuous training (MICT; n = 46); Included 1 hour intervention (warm up 10 min, recreational activities 10 mins, mind body 10 mins, Tai Chi 20 mins, cool down 10 min), 3×/week for 12 months without recovery session
Physical Fitness Tests (BP, HR, Push-ups, sit and reach, one leg standing with eyes closed, vital capacity, height, weight, choice reaction time and hand grip).Visual Analogue Scale (craving level)

Significant changes within-groups for systolic BP, diastolic BP, Vital capacity, hand grip, sit and reach, push-up, one-leg standing with eyes closed and choice reaction time (P.0.001).The craving level also decreased after 12 months for both interventions, no significance found between groups and the interactions of group x time for physical fitness or cravings. Both interventions positively affected physical fitness.
Level 2Experimental study design, randomized with comparative pre and post study typeRisk of Bias Moderate

ParticipantsN = 40Type of Substance:AlcoholInclusion Criteria:Stage 1 or 2 alcohol withdrawal, under 65, no history of recurrent seizures or delirious mental stateIntervention Setting Turning Point Deaddiction Center

Intervention 1: Exercise Group (n = 20); In addition to pharmacological treatment-performed progressive muscle relaxation, deep breathing, balance & coordination, and strengthening exercises. Intensity of exercise progressed over treatment, 5 days/week, 2×/day for 45 days.Control Group: Pharmacological treatment only (n = 20)
Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment of Alcohol (CIWA-Ar; assesses alcohol withdrawal)

Outcomes assessed at pre and post intervention. Within groups, there was a statistically significant difference between pre and posttest for the exercise group (p < .000) and control group (p < .000).Between groups, there was a significant difference in posttest scores (p < .001) indicating more reduction of symptoms in the exercise group with medication.
Music and Dance Interventions
Level 3Controlled Trial without RandomizationRisk of Bias Moderate
ParticipantsN = 20 (M age, 19 to 42 years of age; 100% female)Inclusion CriteriaFemale, referred to program by a behavioral management agencyIntervention Setting Outpatient substance abuse program

Intervention 1: Music therapy program, 6-week program, involving three music therapy interventions (movement-to music activities, rhythm activities, and competitive games). Each intervention was conducted for 1 hour, 2×/week for 2 weeks. Clients reported their emotional state prior to participation in a session, and after the session.
State-Trait Anxiety InventoryNovaco Anger Inventory Short FormJournaling-emotional states (depression, stress, anxiety, anger using Likert scale)

Comparisons of the clients’ scores on the dependent variables for each intervention yielded no statistically significant results, p > .05.A t-test comparison of pretest scores for the clients that remained in the study (n = 10) versus clients that dropped out of the study (n = 10) was conducted. The Novaco Anger Scale was statistically significant for clients remaining in the study (t value of 5.66, p < .001). State anxiety was statistically significant for clients remaining in the study(t score of 2.872, p < .02.)
Level 2Controlled Trial without RandomizationRisk of Bias Moderate
ParticipantsN = 60 (21.7% female)Inclusion CriteriaDSM-IV criteria for substance dependencyIntervention Setting Inpatient detoxification unit

Intervention 1: Dance movement therapy (DMT, n = 20), Chacian techniques to enhance socialization between self and othersIntervention 2: DMT and Guided Imagery (GI; n = 20)Control Group: (n = 20)No intervention
Adult Manifest Anxiety Scale (AMAS)Physiological Anxiety ScaleThe Worry/Oversensitivity ScaleThe Social Concerns/Stress ScaleThe Lie Scale

The anxiety level of each group that received a clinical intervention significantly decreased on all scales. The control group, which received no intervention, showed no significant change, and therefore suggests that those who did not desire change did not experience change.Psychological Anxiety-DMT only = p < .003, DMT & GI = p < .010Worry/Oversensitivity-DMT only = p < .005, DMT & GI = p < .011Social Concerns/Stress-DMT only = p < .002, DMT & GI = .03Lie Scale-DMT only = p = .491, DMT & GI = p = .228Total Anxiety-DMT only = p < .001, DMT & GI = p < .013

fn.p: Note. Includes evidence of physical activity interventions within the scope of occupational therapy in categories of yoga, tai chi, comprehensive integrative approaches, high-intensity and circuit training, and music and dance interventions.
